questions.answer: D. T1 is (c = getchar()) != '\n'; T2 is putchar(c);
questions.to_find: The correct expressions for T1 and T2 so that wrt_it() prints the input string in reverse order.

understand.plain: The function wrt_it() is recursive. It reads a character and, if it is not a newline, calls itself recursively before printing the character. This means characters are printed only when the recursion unwinds — which is in reverse order of reading. T1 must read and check a character, and T2 must print it.

keywords.explain: A function that reads a character, recurses to process the rest, then acts on the character — so the last character read is the first printed.
keywords.example: Input 'AB\n': read A, recurse; read B, recurse; read \n, stop; print B; print A.

keywords.explain: Standard C library function that reads one character from stdin and returns it as an int.
keywords.example: int c = getchar(); // reads next char

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='150' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='85' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>call wrt_it</text><text x='85' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>c = 'A'</text><rect x='200' y='80' width='150' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='275' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>call wrt_it</text><text x='275' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>c = 'B'</text><rect x='390' y='150' width='110' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='445' y='176' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>c = '\n', return</text><line x1='160' y1='40' x2='200' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='350' y1='110' x2='390' y2='170' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='350' y='185' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>unwind: print B, then A</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Call stack diagram showing recursive calls: first reads 'A' and recurses, then reads 'B' and recurses, then reads newline and returns. On unwinding, 'B' is printed first, then 'A', achieving reversal.

given.aim: Fill T1 and T2 in a recursive function so the input string is printed in reverse.

terms.meaning: Recursive function that reads characters and prints them in reverse using the call stack.
terms.connects: T1 controls when to recurse; T2 prints the character on return.

terms.meaning: Must read a character into c and check if it is not a newline, so recursion continues for non-newline chars.
terms.connects: If true, recurse deeper; if false (newline), start unwinding.

terms.meaning: Must print the character c that was read before the recursive call.
terms.connects: Called after recursion unwinds, so characters are printed in reverse order.

given.plan: T1 must both read a character (c = getchar()) and test it against newline. T2 must print c using putchar(c). The recursive structure ensures that printing happens after all deeper calls return, producing reverse output.

steps.title: Determine what T1 must do
steps.apply: T1 must read a character and store it in c, then check if it is not a newline. The expression (c = getchar()) reads a char into c and evaluates to c. If c != '\n', the condition is true and wrt_it() is called recursively. Option A fails because getchar() is called without assigning to c, so the character is lost. Option C fails because c is used before being assigned.
steps.note: Assignment inside the condition is a common C idiom: (c = getchar()) != '\n' reads AND checks in one expression.

steps.title: Determine what T2 must do
steps.apply: After the recursive call returns, T2 prints the character c. putchar(c) is the correct call. Option B uses getchar(c) which is wrong — getchar() takes no arguments. The character c was already read in T1 and is still in scope.
steps.note: Because T2 executes after wrt_it() returns, the last character read (just before the newline) is printed first during unwinding, giving reverse order.

steps.title: Verify with an example
steps.apply: Input 'AB\n': call 1 reads 'A' (c='A'), recurses; call 2 reads 'B' (c='B'), recurses; call 3 reads '\n', T1 false, returns; call 2 resumes: putchar('B'); call 1 resumes: putchar('A'). Output: 'BA'. Correct reversal.
steps.note: Option D is the only one with both correct T1 and correct T2.

solution.result: D. T1 is (c = getchar()) != '\n'; T2 is putchar(c);

questions.formula_note: Recursive reversal using the call stack: read before recursing, print after returning causes LIFO reversal of the input sequence.

questions.to_find: The correct condition E such that the algorithm correctly finds if two distinct elements in a sorted array differ by exactly S.

understand.plain: The array is sorted in increasing order. We use two pointers i and j (i < j always since j starts at 1 and we only increment i or j). We check if a[j] - a[i] equals S. If the difference is too small, we move j forward to increase it. If the difference is too large, we move i forward to decrease it. Condition E decides when to increment j.

keywords.explain: Using two indices that move through a sorted array to find a pair satisfying a condition, without checking all O(n^2) pairs.
keywords.example: i=0,j=1; if diff < S: j++; if diff > S: i++; if diff == S: found.

keywords.explain: In a sorted array a[0] <= a[1] <= ... <= a[n-1]. Increasing j increases the difference a[j]-a[i]; increasing i decreases it.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='30' width='60' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a[0]</text><text x='50' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>i</text><rect x='90' y='30' width='60' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a[1]</text><text x='120' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>j</text><rect x='160' y='30' width='60' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a[2]</text><rect x='230' y='30' width='60' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a[3]</text><rect x='300' y='30' width='60' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>...</text><text x='20' y='120' class='sub ink'>diff < S: j++ (need bigger difference)</text><text x='20' y='140' class='sub ink'>diff > S: i++ (need smaller difference)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array cells with i pointing to a[0] and j pointing to a[1]. Labels show: if diff < S then j++ (increase difference by moving right pointer); if diff > S then i++ (decrease difference by moving left pointer).

given.aim: Find condition E that makes the two-pointer algorithm correctly detect if any pair (a[i], a[j]) has a[j] - a[i] == S in a sorted array.

terms.meaning: The target difference we are looking for; S > 0.
terms.connects: The algorithm compares current difference a[j]-a[i] with S to guide pointer movement.

terms.meaning: Two indices into the sorted array; i < j always, since we only increment them.
terms.example: i=0, j=1 initially; move j right if diff is too small.
terms.connects: Moving j right increases a[j]-a[i]; moving i right decreases it.

terms.meaning: When the current difference is strictly less than S, incrementing j will bring us closer to S.
terms.example: a[j]-a[i] < S means difference too small, so j++.
terms.connects: This is the condition that directs the search toward larger differences.

given.plan: Analyze what each branch of the if-else should do. The else-if handles equality (found). The else branch handles the case where difference is too large (i++). So the if-branch (E) must handle the case where difference is too small (j++).

steps.title: Understand the three cases
steps.apply: The while loop has three cases: (1) if E: j++; (2) else if a[j]-a[i]==S: break (found); (3) else: i++. Since (2) handles equality and (3) handles the remaining case after (1) and (2), case (1) and case (3) must cover a[j]-a[i] < S and a[j]-a[i] > S respectively.
steps.note: The two-pointer approach works because the array is sorted — we can reason about which pointer to move.

steps.title: Determine which case gets j++ and which gets i++
steps.formula_raw: if diff < S -> j++ ; if diff > S -> i++
steps.apply: If a[j]-a[i] < S (difference too small): we need a larger difference, so increase j (move to a larger element on the right). If a[j]-a[i] > S (difference too large): we need a smaller difference, so increase i (move to a larger element on the left, shrinking the gap). Therefore E = (a[j] - a[i] < S).
steps.note: This ensures we explore all candidate pairs efficiently in O(n) time.

steps.title: Verify with an example
steps.note: The answer is B: a[j] - a[i] < S.

questions.formula_note: Two-pointer technique on a sorted array: move the right pointer when difference is below target, move the left pointer when difference is above target, achieving O(n) pair search.
